questions.question: Explain the StringJoiner class in Java and describe its constructors with parameters.
questions.hint: Focus on the delimiter, prefix, and suffix parameters in constructors.
keyPoints: StringJoiner is a class in java.util package used to construct a sequence of characters separated by delimiter
It has three constructors: StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter)
StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence prefix, CharSequence suffix)
Default constructor uses empty prefix and suffix
It implements Iterable interface and is used for joining strings efficiently

questions.question: Compare String.join() method with StringJoiner class in Java. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: String.join() is a static method while StringJoiner is a class
String.join() takes delimiter and array/Iterable as parameters
StringJoiner allows adding elements dynamically using add() method
StringJoiner supports prefix and suffix while String.join() does not
StringJoiner is preferred when building strings incrementally or with custom prefix/suffix

questions.question: Write a Java code snippet to join an array of strings ['Java', 'Python', 'C++'] using StringJoiner with delimiter '-' and enclosing brackets [ ].
questions.hint: Remember that prefix and suffix are added only when elements are present.
keyPoints: Import java.util.StringJoiner
Create StringJoiner object with '-' delimiter, '[' prefix, ']' suffix
Use add() method to add each array element
Call toString() to get the final result
Output should be: [Java-Python-C++]

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the merge() method in StringJoiner class with an example.
questions.hint: Note that merge() differs from add() in handling prefix/suffix.
keyPoints: merge() method adds the content of another StringJoiner without its prefix and suffix
It is used to combine two StringJoiner objects
The prefix of the incoming StringJoiner is not added, but suffix is added to merged content
Useful for combining partial string sequences

questions.hint: Remember prefix and suffix are always included in output.
keyPoints: First output: () - empty StringJoiner with prefix and suffix only
Second output: (A, B) - elements added with delimiter
Prefix and suffix appear even when empty if setEmptyValue() is not used
toString() returns the constructed string including prefix, suffix, and delimiters

questions.question: Explain in detail the StringJoiner class, its constructors, important methods, and demonstrate with a complete example program showing how to create comma-separated and formatted output.
questions.hint: Cover all constructors and methods with practical examples.
keyPoints: StringJoiner is in java.util package introduced in Java 8
Constructors: StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter) and StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence prefix, CharSequence suffix)
Methods: add(), merge(), setEmptyValue(), length(), toString()
add() appends element with delimiter
merge() combines two StringJoiners without duplicating prefix
setEmptyValue() sets output when no elements added
Example showing List to CSV conversion and formatted output

questions.question: Design a Java program to create a comma-separated values (CSV) line from a list of employee names and salaries using StringJoiner. Include error handling for empty lists.
questions.hint: Consider using two StringJoiners - one for header and one for data rows.
keyPoints: Import required classes: java.util.StringJoiner, java.util.List
Create StringJoiner with comma delimiter
Loop through employee data and add each field
Handle empty list scenario with setEmptyValue() or null check
Show output format: Name,Salary (header) followed by data rows
Demonstrate with sample employee data

questions.question: Compare String concatenation using '+' operator, StringBuilder, String.join(), and StringJoiner. Discuss performance implications and memory considerations.
questions.hint: Consider time complexity and memory allocation for each approach.
keyPoints: Concatenation with '+' creates new String objects in each iteration (inefficient)
StringBuilder is mutable and efficient for single-threaded environments
String.join() is static utility method for joining arrays/Iterables
StringJoiner provides delimiter, prefix, suffix control with efficient internal implementation
Performance: StringJoiner and StringBuilder are better than '+' for loops
StringJoiner internally uses StringBuilder
StringJoiner is preferred for formatted output with delimiters

questions.question: Explain the internal working of String.join() method in Java. How does it handle null elements? Write a program to demonstrate joining array elements and handling nulls.
questions.hint: Test with null elements to show NullPointerException behavior.
keyPoints: String.join() is a static method in java.lang.String class
Syntax: String.join(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence... elements)
It uses StringJoiner internally for the joining operation
It throws NullPointerException if any element is null (from Java 8)
In Java 11+, String.join() can handle null delimiter but not null elements
Demonstrate with array containing null elements showing exception

questions.hint: String.join() is optimized and doesn't create unnecessary intermediate objects.
keyPoints: Output: university-Belgaum-2024
One new String object created for the result (due to join operation)
The array elements are not new objects (they reference existing strings)
No intermediate StringBuilder is visible as String.join() optimizes internally

questions.hint: Remember merge() doesn't add the prefix of the merged StringJoiner.
keyPoints: Stage 1: [No Data] - setEmptyValue is used when empty
Stage 2: [A] - prefix added, one element, no delimiter after it
Stage 3: [A:B:C] - delimiter added between elements
Stage 4: [A:B:C-X-Y] - merge adds sj2 content without its prefix/suffix

questions.question: Calculate and compare the number of String objects created when joining 5 strings using: (a) concatenation with '+' in a loop, (b) StringJoiner. Explain the difference.
questions.hint: For n elements with concatenation, complexity is O(n²) in terms of character copies.
keyPoints: With '+' in loop: n*(n+1)/2 + 1 = 5*6/2 + 1 = 16 String objects approximately
Each iteration creates new String object copying all previous content
With StringJoiner: Only 1 final String object is created
StringJoiner uses internal StringBuilder to accumulate characters
Memory difference is significant for large datasets

questions.question: What is the purpose of setEmptyValue() method in StringJoiner? Write code to demonstrate its usage.
questions.hint: This method is called before any elements are added.
keyPoints: setEmptyValue() sets the string to return when no elements have been added
It accepts a CharSequence parameter
Default empty value is empty string ""
Useful for displaying placeholder when list is empty
Only used when toString() is called on empty StringJoiner

questions.question: Given a List<String> cities = Arrays.asList("Bangalore", "Mysore", "Hubli"); Write the output and explain the result of: String.join(" -> ", cities);
keyPoints: Output: Bangalore -> Mysore -> Hubli
String.join() iterates through the Iterable
Each element is separated by the specified delimiter
No prefix or suffix is added
Works with any Iterable implementation

questions.question: Design a Java program to generate SQL IN clause from a list of IDs using StringJoiner. For example, input list {101, 102, 103, 104} should produce output: (101, 102, 103, 104).
questions.hint: StringJoiner is ideal for generating SQL clauses as it handles delimiters automatically.
keyPoints: Import java.util.StringJoiner and java.util.List
Create StringJoiner with comma delimiter, opening parenthesis '(' and closing ')'
Iterate through list of IDs and add each to StringJoiner
Use toString() to get final SQL IN clause
Handle empty list case
Show how this is more efficient than string concatenation for large lists
